I graze upon the bioluminescent moss of the orbital gardens, oblivious to the silent screams of the dying star systems beyond the glass dome.
I leap through the ruins of the High Chancellor's atrium, my hooves clicking against mosaics of a dynasty that forgot how to feed its people.
I watch from the edge of the holographic forest as the last transport ships ignite their engines, leaving me as the sole witness to a world falling into shadow.
I scent the metallic tang of ozone and decay drifting from the rusted ventilation shafts of the planetary spire.
I tilt my head to listen to the distant thunder of planetary shields collapsing, thinking it is merely a storm rolling across the velvet plains.
I froze in the boardroom like a startled buck when the CEO revealed the hostile takeover bid.
I feel like a deer in the headlights as the auditors scrutinize every discrepancy in my quarterly reports.
I navigated the shark-infested waters of the negotiation table with the cautious grace of a doe.
I spent the weekend in the wilderness of my own anxiety, feeling as fragile as a fawn amidst the corporate giants.
I locked eyes with my rival across the conference table, two stags clashing for dominance over the new empire.
I watch the humans scream and clash their stones in the valley, my ears twitching at the chaos that disrupts my quiet grazing.
I feel the sharp sting of the flint arrow piercing my flank as the hunters of the northern clan pursue me through the brush.
I freeze beneath the ancient ferns, smelling the metallic scent of blood and the musk of angry men drifting on the wind.
I leap over the rushing creek to escape the shouting warriors who fight over who owns the forest where I dwell.
I gaze with wide eyes at the painted skins of the victors, knowing they will soon carve my meat to celebrate their conquest.
I graze upon the holographic meadows of the Central Plaza, tasting the ozone and static of the city's dreaming subconscious.
I weave through the humming currents of the mag-lev transit lines, a ghost of organic velvet amidst a torrent of chrome.
I watch the sentient skyscrapers shift their geometry to follow the artificial sun, wondering why they cannot simply bow their heads to the wind.
I leave cloven prints in the shimmering nanite dust of the lower wards, marking a trail of wilderness through a grid of perfect logic.
I listen to the rhythmic pulse of the city's core processor and feel it syncopate with the frantic drumming of my own wild heart.
I stand before the spectral judge, my hooves clicking nervously against the cold marble of the astral floor.
I cannot find the words to plead my case, for the legal jargon of the afterlife is a language my wild heart cannot speak.
The jury consists of shimmering ghosts who watch me with hollow eyes, weighing my innocence against the laws of the forest.
I feel the heavy gaze of the prosecutor as he presents the blood-stained brush as evidence of my transgression.
I bow my antlered head in submission, waiting for the final verdict to decide if my soul shall return to the green woods or fade into the void.
I watch the flickering emergency lights cast long, rhythmic shadows across the rusted bulkheads of the sleeping quarters.
I taste the metallic tang of saltwater leaking through the hairline fractures in the reinforced observation dome.
I tread softly over the shattered glass and scattered data pads that litter the flooded laboratory floor.
I listen to the distant, rhythmic thrum of a failing geothermal generator vibrating through the titanium deck.
I sniff the stale, recycled air of the hydroponics bay where bioluminescent algae have overgrown the dead circuitry.
I stand motionless amidst the stark white void of the gallery, wondering why these humans stare at my velvet antlers as if they were sculptures carved from frozen silence.
I find the aggressive minimalism of the concrete walls suffocating, longing instead for the chaotic geometry of a bramble thicket.
I watch the critics whisper in hushed tones about the "juxtaposition of nature and industry," unaware that I am merely searching for a single blade of grass in this sterile hall.
I feel the jarring vibration of the experimental noise soundtrack rattling my ribs, a sound far more predatory than any wolf I have ever encountered.
I observe the flashing strobe lights reflecting in the eyes of the patrons, turning this curated space into a disorienting forest of artificial lightning.
I watch from the forest edge as the village bell tolls incessantly for the dead, wondering why the humans are suddenly so still.
I step cautiously over the discarded masks of plague doctors, my hooves clicking against the cobblestones of a silent, empty market.
I feast upon the lush grass growing through the cracks of an abandoned manor, undisturbed by the ghosts of the nobility who once hunted me.
I tilt my head in confusion as I find a heap of blackened, linen-wrapped bodies left in the road, smelling of vinegar and rot.
I flee back into the deep thicket when I see the red crosses painted on the doors, sensing a sickness in the air that tastes of copper and ash.
I stand frozen in the meadow, watching the forest textures flicker and peel away to reveal a void of scrolling green code.
My hooves click against the grass, but the sound repeats in a perfect, haunting loop every three seconds.
I tried to leap over the creek, but I clipped through the terrain and am now falling through an endless gray expanse.
I see my reflection in the pond, but my antlers are vibrating violently, oscillating between a dozen different shapes.
A hunter fires at me, yet the bullet freezes mid-air as the sky turns a flat, neon purple and the wind ceases to exist.
